result.coverage_notes:
Audited the EdDSA/Schnorr 2p+mp cluster in full: src/cbmpc/protocol/eddsa.cpp, schnorr_2p.cpp, schnorr_mp.cpp, anchored to public API headers include/cbmpc/api/schnorr_2p.h, schnorr_mp.h, eddsa_2p.h, eddsa_mp.h. Followed verification primitives into include-internal/cbmpc/internal/crypto/commitment.h and src/cbmpc/zk/zk_ec.cpp (uc_batch_dl_finite_difference_impl_t prove/verify), plus bn_t::rand (base_bn.cpp -> BN_rand_range).

Checked each value RECEIVED from a counterparty for missing verification:

1. Nonce commitment binding (2p): P1 commits to R1 (commitment_t bound to sid1+pid, schnorr_2p.cpp:40-42) BEFORE seeing P2's R2 (sent at :51). P2 opens P1's commitment at :66. Standard commit-first ordering; P1 is bound and cannot adapt R1 to R2. No malleability.

2. Fiat-Shamir / FS challenge binding: nonce-point DL is proven via UC Fischlin batch-DL zk (zk_ec.cpp:244 and :172) whose common_hash binds (G, Q=statement, R, session_id, aux=party_index). Verify (zk_ec.cpp:233-263) performs on-curve checks on Q and R, right-open-range check on z, recomputes R_test = z*G - horner_poly(PQ,e), and requires the Fischlin hash to be zero. Statement-bound, transcript-bound, and party-index domain-separated (aux=1 for P1, 2 for P2 in 2p; aux=j in mp). No reusable/replayable/malleable proof path found.

3. sid agreement: 2p sid = sha256(sid1,sid2) with both contributions cross-bound; mp sid_i = sha256(all sid_i contributions) with per-peer equality checks (schnorr_mp.cpp:91-104). h consistency hash (schnorr_mp.cpp:69-70,94-95) binds msgs, Q, Qis, and the round-1 commitments across all parties.

4. Nonce quality: ki/k1/k2 = bn_t::rand(q) -> OpenSSL BN_rand_range (unbiased, CSPRNG), fresh per signature; no reuse or bias path.

5. Share secrecy: in 2p, P2 sends s2 = e*x2 + k2 first (:106); P1 never reveals s1; k2 random so P1 learns nothing of x2, and P2 sees no s1 so learns nothing of x1. In mp, only the sig_receiver sums partial ssi; aggregate signature is verified (verify_key.verify) before output, so a deviating party can only cause an abort.

6. Deserialization/length: batch-size checks present on all received vectors (schnorr_2p.cpp:52-53,64,109; schnorr_mp.cpp:105-106,156-157). EdDSA/BIP340 msg-size and curve checks present.

The only adversarial deviations available to a malicious party result in a verification abort (E_CRYPTO), i.e. DoS/abort, with no secret-dependent leakage on abort. This does not meet the Medium+ bar (key extraction / forgery via public API). Conclusion: no eligible vulnerability found in this cluster. Zero findings is the honest result for this well-audited code.

Audited the PVE cluster in full: include/cbmpc/api/pve_base_pke.h, pve_batch_ac.h, pve_batch_single_recipient.h (entry points) and implementations src/cbmpc/protocol/pve.cpp, pve_ac.cpp, pve_base.cpp, pve_batch.cpp, src/cbmpc/crypto/elgamal.cpp, plus API wiring in src/cbmpc/api/pve_batch_ac.cpp and the internal headers (include-internal/.../protocol/pve*.h), constants in include-internal/.../crypto/base.h, and point/subgroup validation in src/cbmpc/crypto/base_ecc.cpp / base_eddsa.cpp / ec25519_core.cpp.

Soundness analysis of the verifiable-encryption proof (the cluster focus): The scheme is a kappa=128-row cut-and-choose. For each row the encryptor commits to (c0,c1,X0,X1) and the Fiat-Shamir challenge b = hash_string(Q,label,c0,c1,[quorum_c0,quorum_c1,]X0,X1) selects which half is opened. I verified that:
- ec_pve_t::verify (pve.cpp:70-116), ec_pve_batch_t::verify (pve_batch.cpp:74-142), and ec_pve_ac_t::verify (pve_ac.cpp:136-193) each recompute the opened half deterministically (re-encrypt with the verifier-supplied ek, so ek is implicitly bound through c0/c1), take the unopened half from stored values, recompute b_tag independently, and reject on b_tag != b. The challenge is bound to Q, label, all ciphertexts and all points, so the transcript is complete and the proof is non-malleable in the relevant sense.
- Bit-width / length checks on attacker-controlled deserialized rows are present before use (pve.cpp:88-89,124,130-131; pve_batch.cpp:107-108,117,159,162,165,168; pve_ac.cpp:176,274,278), preventing the bi-dependent r/x_bin confusion.
- Decryption enforces an independent consistency check x*G == Q (pve.cpp:140; pve_batch.cpp:183; pve_ac.cpp:285), so even though decrypt/combine run with skip_verify (documented), a cheating encryptor cannot make an undecryptable/wrong plaintext pass to a wrong Q.
- The public-API layer validates curve membership and Q via curve.check/is_in_subgroup (verify_ac pve_batch_ac.cpp:185-194; Ed25519 has a genuine torsion-free subgroup check in ec25519_core.cpp:862), Q-count vs batch_count, MAX_BATCH_COUNT bounds, ciphertext/blob size caps, leaf-key set exact-match, and quorum share size == curve order size (combine_ac:340-343).
- AC quorum-share secret sharing: a malicious encryptor placing garbage quorum ciphertexts on unopened rows is caught either by the cut-and-choose (1/2 per row) or by AES-GCM auth failure / x*G!=Q at reconstruction.
- elgamal.cpp is a commitment helper not on the PVE decrypt/verify path; its constant-time assertion is gated to non-vartime scopes.

I looked specifically for: weak/missing FS transcript binding, reusable challenges, malleable proofs, missing on-curve/subgroup checks, missing length/range checks enabling memory unsafety or row confusion, and decryptor-side acceptance of an inconsistent plaintext. None were exploitable through the in-scope public APIs. Out-of-scope demo-* and c_api paths were not considered. Honest result: zero defensible findings for this cluster.

Audited the ECDSA-MP cluster end-to-end: public API include/cbmpc/api/ecdsa_mp.h (dkg_additive/dkg_ac/refresh_additive/refresh_ac/sign_additive/sign_ac) and implementations src/cbmpc/protocol/ecdsa_mp.cpp and src/cbmpc/protocol/ec_dkg.cpp, following helpers into include-internal (mpc_job.h, zk_ec.h, zk_elgamal_com.h) and src/cbmpc/zk/zk_ec.cpp and src/cbmpc/crypto/secret_sharing.cpp.

Key boundary fact: job_mp_t::plain_broadcast (mpc_job.h:307) is NOT an agreement/echo broadcast - it is point-to-point group_message(all,all). Malicious-security therefore depends entirely on per-round consistency hashes. I verified these are present and correct:
- Additive DKG (ec_dkg.cpp key_share_mp_t::dkg): committed-broadcast pattern. Each party commits to Qi (commitment_t with sid_i+pid) in round 1 BEFORE openings in round 2; h=hash(c._js) agreement check (line 152), commitment opened (154), and UC-DL proof pi verified (157). uc_dl_t::verify (zk_ec.cpp:55) performs curve.check(Q) on-curve/subgroup and binds G,Q,A,session_id,aux into the Fiat-Shamir hash. No Q-bias / rogue-key gap found.
- Additive refresh (ec_dkg.cpp:167): checks x_share*G==Qi and SUM(Qis)==Q on input; agreement via h_consistency and h; each R._j[l] (l!=j, includes l==i) proven via batch uc_dl; P2P share bound to broadcast point via r._ji*G==R._j[i] (line 232); invariant new_key.x_share*G==Qi and SUM==Q re-checked.
- AC DKG/refresh (ec_dkg.cpp:264): ac.validate_tree, enough_for_quorum, commitment open of Rs+proof, uc_batch_dl verify, and the strong verify_share_against_ancestors_pub_data (secret_sharing.cpp:344) which checks si*G against the public polynomial at every ancestor node (LEAF/OR equality, AND sum, THRESHOLD lagrange_interpolate_exponent); reconstruct_exponent re-checks Q and curve.check on all shares.
- Signing (ecdsa_mp.cpp:37): ot_role_map fully validated for OOB/antisymmetry; input consistency h=hash(msg,Q,Qis); all ElGamal-commitment ZK proofs (uc_elgamal_com, elgamal_com_mult, uc_elgamal_com_mult_private_scalar, dh, elgamal_com_pub_share_equ) verified with full statement+sid+distinct aux indices; v_theta range-checked (line 291); W==Z.R checks; and crucially the final signature is verified with pub.verify(msg,sig) on the receiver (line 482), catching any residual deviation. The all-to-one beta/rho_k opening is bound by pi_R_eB/pi_R_eRHO_K against locally-recomputed eB[j] derived from already-proven commitments.

Both supported curves (secp256k1, p256) have cofactor 1, so on-curve check implies prime-order subgroup membership; no small-subgroup vector. Fiat-Shamir hashes (ro::hash_string) bind the generator, statement, session_id and a per-proof aux label preventing proof reuse across rounds/parties.

Conclusion: I did not find a vulnerability that is both reachable through the in-scope public APIs and exploitable by a malicious party at the protocol boundary. Every value received from a counterparty is verified before use (commitment binding, ZK proof with curve and transcript binding, P2P-vs-broadcast consistency, and final ECDSA verification). Reporting zero findings rather than a speculative claim. Areas I could not exhaustively prove correct by reading alone (would need dynamic PoC): the internal soundness of the Fischlin transform parameters and the PVW OT-extension MtA correctness in ot.cpp/fischlin.cpp - these are plausible deeper targets but I saw no concrete missing check.

findings.title: combine_additive/combine do not verify that the supplied public shares reconstruct the group public key Q
findings.missing_check: Neither combine_additive (no check that sum(Qi) == pub_key.Q) nor combine (no check that reconstruct_exponent(Qi) == pub_key.Q) verifies that the caller-supplied public shares are consistent with the public key embedded in/validated against pub_key. The decryption-share DLEQ proof only binds each Xi to its corresponding Qi, never to Q.
findings.data_flow: api::tdh2::combine_additive -> crypto::tdh2::combine_additive (curve.check on each Qi, ciphertext.verify, per-share check_partial_decryption_helper(Qi[rid-1]) which proves Xi=x_i*R1 vs Qi=x_i*G) -> V = sum Xi -> ciphertext_t::decrypt(V): key = ro::hash_string(V). The relation V == r*Q (= P used at encryption) is assumed but never enforced; it holds only if sum(Qi)==Q, which is unchecked.
findings.attacker_role: A party that supplies the public_shares / partial decryptions vector to the honest combiner (e.g. a malicious participant feeding the combiner mismatched-but-internally-consistent Qi/Xi from a different sharing).
findings.impact: Produces an incorrect decryption key V; AES-GCM tag verification then fails and the API returns a clean E_CRYPTO error. No key material is leaked and no forged plaintext is accepted (GCM integrity catches it). Effect is limited to availability/robustness and only when the combiner's own inputs are inconsistent. No confidentiality or integrity break demonstrated.
findings.poc_sketch: Run dkg_additive to get genuine (public_key, public_shares, private_shares). Call partial_decrypt on each share for a valid ciphertext, then call combine_additive but pass a permuted/altered public_shares set whose sum != Q together with matching partials; observe E_CRYPTO from the GCM stage rather than a clean 'inconsistent shares' error. Demonstrates the missing pre-check, not a secrecy break.

findings.title: Decryption-share DLEQ challenge omits the statement (R1, Qi, bases) and any domain separator from the Fiat-Shamir hash
findings.missing_check: The Chaum-Pedersen-style decryption-share proof computes ei = ro::hash_number(Xi, Yi, Zi) and verifies with the same hash, but the challenge does not bind the statement points R1 (ciphertext) and Qi (public share), the bases G/R1, a domain-separation tag, nor the ciphertext/label/session id. Contrast with zk_elgamal_com.cpp which hashes the full statement plus session_id.
findings.data_flow: private_share_t::decrypt (api partial_decrypt): ei = hash_number(Xi,Yi,Zi); fi = si + x*ei -> partial_decryption_t::check_partial_decryption_helper (api combine_*): recompute Yi=fi*R1-ei*Xi, Zi=fi*G-ei*Qi, check ei == hash_number(Xi,Yi,Zi).
findings.attacker_role: Malicious decryptor producing a partial-decryption proof for an honest combiner.
findings.impact: No concrete soundness break found: because the verifier recomputes Yi and Zi from R1 and Qi, a transcript only verifies for the exact (R1, Qi) it was produced against, so cross-statement replay/forgery does not succeed and Xi remains pinned to the genuine Qi. The omission is a hardening/spec-deviation concern (defense-in-depth, future-proofing against multi-statement grinding and proof reuse) rather than an exploitable vulnerability.
findings.code_excerpt: ei = ro::hash_number(Xi, Yi, Zi).mod(q);  // statement (R1, Qi, bases, label, sid) not bound
findings.poc_sketch: No working exploit: attempts to reuse a (Xi,ei,fi) transcript against a different ciphertext (different R1) or different Qi fail verification because the recomputed Yi/Zi change, changing ei_test. Reported as Info for transcript-binding hardening.

result.coverage_notes: Read in full: include/cbmpc/api/tdh2.h, src/cbmpc/api/tdh2.cpp, src/cbmpc/crypto/tdh2.cpp, include-internal/cbmpc/internal/crypto/tdh2.h, include-internal/cbmpc/internal/crypto/ro.h; plus ecurve_t::check (base_ecc.cpp) and reconstruct_exponent (secret_sharing.cpp). The TDH2 cluster is well-hardened against the standard malicious-participant attacks: (1) ciphertext_t::verify is called BEFORE any partial decryption is produced (private_share_t::decrypt:72) and inside both combine paths, preventing the classic 'decryption share for malformed ciphertext leaks the share' CCA attack; (2) all received points get curve.check (on-curve, in-subgroup, non-infinity) and scalars get q.is_in_range; (3) label and iv are bound into the ciphertext NIZK challenge e=hash(c,label,R1,W1,R2,W2,iv); (4) Gamma is deterministically derived from (Q,sid) and re-checked in verify and validate_public_key; (5) the API layer validates public keys, bounds blob sizes (MAX_CIPHERTEXT/OPAQUE_BLOB_SIZE), rejects ed25519, dedups party names, and binds rid in [1,n]. The decryption-share DLEQ, although its FS challenge omits the statement, remains sound because the verifier recomputes Yi/Zi from R1 and Qi, pinning the transcript to a single statement. I did NOT find a reachable Medium+ vulnerability: no key-share extraction, no decryption forgery, no missing verification that a malicious counterparty can exploit across the public-API boundary. The two findings are honest low/info-grade observations (a missing sum(Qi)==Q consistency pre-check that only yields clean GCM-tag failures, and a weak-FS hardening gap with no demonstrated exploit). c_api and demo-* were excluded per scope. An honest assessment: this cluster appears well-audited.

Audited the in-scope 2P-ECDSA cluster: include/cbmpc/api/ecdsa_2p.h and include/cbmpc/api/hd_keyset_ecdsa_2p.h, their API shims src/cbmpc/api/ecdsa2pc.cpp and src/cbmpc/api/hd_keyset_ecdsa_2p.cpp, and the implementations src/cbmpc/protocol/ecdsa_2p.cpp, src/cbmpc/protocol/hd_keyset_ecdsa_2p.cpp, src/cbmpc/protocol/ec_dkg.cpp. Followed into include-internal zk_ec.cpp (dh_t/uc_dl_t/uc_batch_dl_t) and zk_paillier.cpp (two_paillier_equal, valid_paillier).

CONCLUSION: No defensible, publicly-API-reachable vulnerability found. The malicious-boundary checks are present and correct in every place I examined:

DKG (ecdsa2pc::dkg, ec_dkg::dkg_2p_t): standard commit-first DKG. P1 commits to Q1 (com.gen), P2 sends Q2 with a UC-DL proof (pi_2) verified before P1 opens, P1 opens Q1 and proves pi_1; step3/step4 call curve.check on received Q1/Q2 and pi.verify (which itself does curve.check). Paillier setup (paillier_gen_interactive_t::step4_p2_output) verifies modulus size bounds, create_pub, verify_cipher (GCD), valid_paillier, PDL (binds c_key to dlog of Q1), paillier-pedersen-equal and range proofs before P2 accepts c_key. Sound.

Signing (sign_batch_impl, default/in-scope mode): commitment on (msgs,R1,pi_1) bound and opened; R1/R2 validated via uc_batch_dl verify (on-curve+subgroup); batch-size consistency checks on R1/R2/c/zk_ecdsa guard deserialization. The integer-commit ZK proof (zk_ecdsa_sign_2pc_integer_commit_t::verify) gates P1's Paillier decryption and binds pai_c to c_key=Enc(x1)+q<<SEC_P_STAT, to R2, m, r, with curve.check on Q2/R2/G_tag/Q2_tag and range checks on all responses; Fiat-Shamir challenge hashes N,c_key,c,Q2,R2,m,r, all commitments, C_enc_tag, sid and aux (full statement + domain separation). After ZK, P1 runs verify_cipher and re-verifies the final ECDSA signature against Q before output. A malformed c that passed ZK cannot yield a valid signature.

The SIGN_MODE_GLOBAL_ABORT path (which skips the per-signature ZK proof and carries the E_ECDSA_2P_BIT_LEAK single-bit leakage) is NOT exposed: src/cbmpc/api/ecdsa2pc.cpp::sign hard-wires coinbase::mpc::ecdsa2pc::sign (SIGN_MODE_DEFAULT). sign_with_global_abort has no public-API entry point, so its bit-leak is out of scope for this cluster.

Refresh: P2 setting p0_valid_key/p0_no_small_factors/c0_plaintext_range=verified for the two_paillier_equal proof is justified because the OLD key/c_key were verified during the original DKG and persisted in P2's own blob (re-checked at deserialize via verify_cipher); the NEW key undergoes valid_paillier + bounds + verify_cipher before acceptance; rho1 committed by P1 and opened after P2 commits rho2. Sound.

HD derive_keys (VRF-Compute-2P): Z1/Z2 each accompanied by a dh_t proof bound to (P, other_K_share, Z) and sid/aux; dh_t::verify does curve.check on all three points. delta is derived from Z=Z1+Z2 by RO hash, so neither party controls it unilaterally. Sound.

Blob boundary (ecdsa2pc.cpp blob_to_key / attach_private_scalar): role<->paillier-private consistency, N bit-size floor, x_share in Z_N, verify_cipher, and (for P1) decrypt(c_key)==x_share mod N; attach validates x*G==Qi_self and Q on-curve. Robust.

INFORMATIONAL (not reported as a finding): refresh() line 233 performs new_key.x_share = key.x_share + rho as a non-constant-time BN_add on a growing integer representative; the code documents this and the leakage (limb-length/refresh-count, not the secret scalar mod q) is only meaningful to a local co-resident attacker, outside the network threat model and not reachable as a crypto break through the public API. Not eligible.

result.reason: Confirmed against src/cbmpc/crypto/tdh2.cpp:89 (prover) and :104-125 (verifier). The decryption-share proof is a Chaum-Pedersen equality-of-discrete-logs proof: ei=hash(Xi,Yi,Zi), fi=si+x*ei. The verifier recomputes Yi=fi*R1-ei*Xi and Zi=fi*G-ei*Qi from the GENUINE R1 (the ciphertext is independently verified via ciphertext.verify before any share is checked, so R1 is authentic) and the GENUINE Qi (taken from the combiner's trusted public-share set, indexed by rid, never from the prover). Although the Fiat-Shamir challenge omits R1, Qi, the bases, and a domain/session tag, Yi and Zi functionally encode those values, so any transcript verifies only against the exact (R1,Qi) it was produced for. Soundness of the equality-of-DL proof forces Xi=x*R1 with the same x as Qi=x*G; a malicious decryptor cannot produce a verifying proof for a wrong/forged share, cannot cause cross-ciphertext (different R1) or cross-share (different Qi) replay to succeed, and gains no grinding/forgery/DoS advantage. The candidate itself states no concrete soundness break exists. This is a defense-in-depth/spec-deviation hardening note, not a reachable protocol-boundary exploit.
result.attack_at_boundary: No exploitable attack at the public API boundary. A malicious decryptor calling partial_decrypt and feeding a crafted partial_decryption_t to combine_additive/combine cannot make an honest combiner accept any Xi other than x*R1, because the equality-of-DL proof recomputes Yi,Zi from the genuine R1 and trusted Qi. No secret leak, forgery, or DoS results.

result.reason: The code in src/cbmpc/crypto/tdh2.cpp:127-178 matches the finding's technical description: combine_additive sums Xi into V and combine reconstructs V via reconstruct_exponent, and neither verifies that the caller-supplied public shares Qi reconstruct the group key Q (no sum(Qi)==Q or reconstruct(Qi)==Q check). The per-share check_partial_decryption_helper only proves a DLEQ relation binding Xi to its own Qi, not to Q. So the missing check is real. However, under the concrete protocol-boundary attack lens the finding does not survive. The decryption key is ro::hash_string(V) fed into aes_gcm_t::decrypt, which is authenticated decryption (decrypt at tdh2.cpp:94-102 returns error on any tag mismatch). If a malicious participant supplies internally-consistent-but-wrong (Qi, Xi) pairs, V != r*Q, the AES-GCM key is wrong, and the tag check fails, returning a clean E_CRYPTO error. There is no confidentiality break (GCM is all-or-nothing; a wrong key leaks no plaintext), no integrity/forgery break (the ciphertext author's GCM tag is the integrity gate and is independent of V's correctness), and no escalation of DoS (a malicious party can already force decryption failure by submitting garbage shares, which the DLEQ check rejects; supplying consistent-but-wrong shares merely shifts the rejection from the DLEQ check to the GCM tag check, producing the identical safe failure). The finding itself concedes all of this: AES-GCM catches the wrong key, no key material leaks, no forged plaintext is accepted. The missing check is therefore a defense-in-depth/robustness observation, not an exploitable soundness vulnerability — GCM provides the real integrity binding on the recovered plaintext. No secret, forgery, or meaningful DoS results from the honest combiner running unmodified code.
result.attack_at_boundary: A malicious participant supplies the honest combiner with internally-consistent but Q-inconsistent (Qi, Xi) partial-decryption pairs (valid DLEQ for an x_i unrelated to the real sharing). The per-share DLEQ check passes, but V = sum(Xi) != r*Q, so the derived AES-GCM key is wrong and aes_gcm_t::decrypt fails the tag check, returning E_CRYPTO. Outcome: a benign decryption failure, identical to submitting outright garbage shares.

result.coverage_notes:
Read in full: drbg.cpp, ro.cpp, secret_sharing.cpp, lagrange.cpp, base_mod.cpp, base_paillier.cpp, base_ecc.cpp, base_ec_core.cpp, base_ecc_secp256k1.cpp, plus ed25519 subgroup code in ec25519_core.cpp and zk_ec.cpp (Fiat-Shamir consumer). Anchored to public APIs under include/cbmpc/api/ (ecdsa_2p/mp, eddsa_2p/mp, schnorr_2p/mp, tdh2, pve_*, hd_keyset_*).

No eligible finding. Detail on each candidate examined and why rejected:

1. drbg.cpp gen_bn256() fast path (lines 56-69) intentionally skips reduction mod q for near-2^256 moduli (true for secp256k1 order). This would produce scalars >= q with non-negligible probability (~2^-128 to small). HOWEVER, grep shows gen_bn256 has NO callers anywhere in src/ or include-internal/ outside its own declaration in base.h:133. It is dead code from the in-scope-API perspective; not reachable. If it were ever wired into nonce/challenge generation it would be a real bias bug, but it is not currently reachable.

2. ro.cpp drbg_sample_number(mem_t, int m) (lines 27-34) uses biased `be_get_8 % m`. Explicitly documented as "deterministic non-cryptographic choices" and routed only via hash_number_t::mod(int). No in-scope ZK/protocol caller uses the int-modulus form; cryptographic challenges use mod(const mod_t&) which samples bits+SEC_P_STAT(64) then reduces (negligible bias). Rejected.

3. ro.cpp hash_numbers_t::mod256 — uses mod_bits + SEC_P_COM(128) statistical margin and bounded copy with cb_assert guards; no in-scope caller (grep: only ro.cpp/ro.h reference mod256). Not reachable.

4. base_paillier.cpp: received ciphertexts are gated by verify_cipher()/batch_verify_ciphers() (open-range in (0,N^2) + coprimality with N). decrypt() asserts src in (0,N^2) and coprime. update_private vartime on secret primes is documented offline-setup-only and operand-length fixed for RSA-2048. paillier_t::convert deserialization rebuilds public state via mod_t::convert (validates modulus) and explicitly defers higher-level validation to callers; private-key path is for trusted local blobs only. No malicious-counterparty gap found at the protocol boundary.

5. EC point validation: ecc_point_t::convert_fixed_curve (base_ecc.cpp:737-762) calls curve.check() on every deserialized point = valid + correct curve + is_in_subgroup + non-infinity (unless allow_ecc_infinity scope). secp256k1/OSSL is_in_subgroup==is_on_curve is correct (cofactor 1). ed25519 (cofactor 8) has a genuine subgroup test via (q-1)*P == -P (ec25519_core.cpp:862-867). reconstruct_exponent validates all share points before use. No missing subgroup/on-curve check found.

6. zk_ec.cpp uc_dl: Fiat-Shamir hash binds G, Q, A, session_id, aux; verify() re-checks Q and all A[i] on curve and z[i] in [0,q). Transcript binding looks complete.

7. secret_sharing.cpp ac deserialization (convert_node) builds a tree from untrusted length-prefixed input with recursion; potential unbounded-recursion stack exhaustion is at most a Low non-crypto DoS and its reachability through a public API message boundary was not established within scope.

Conclusion: This primitives cluster is well-audited; the suspicious-looking constructs are documented, dead, or confined to trusted setup. Honest answer is zero eligible findings.

Audited the deserialization-of-untrusted-protocol-messages cluster in full. Files read completely: src/cbmpc/core/convert.cpp, buf.cpp, buf128.cpp, buf256.cpp, plus the deserialization framework include-internal/cbmpc/internal/core/convert.h, and the primitive convert() paths in crypto/base_bn.cpp, base_bn256.cpp, base_ecc.cpp. Followed into protocol deser/round handling: protocol/ec_dkg.cpp, ecdsa_mp.cpp, pve.cpp (and pve.h struct layout), with spot checks of ecdsa_2p/eddsa/schnorr message indexing. Anchored entry points to include/cbmpc/api/* (ecdsa_mp.h, ecdsa_2p.h, eddsa_*, schnorr_*, pve_*, tdh2.h) via the ser/deser<> helpers and mpc_job message round-trips.

Conclusion: no reportable, public-API-reachable deserialization/memory-safety vulnerability found. The (de)serialization layer is uniformly hardened against the requested cluster:

- converter_t::at_least() and forward() contain explicit signedness and integer-overflow guards (n<0, offset<0, offset>size, n>size-offset, offset>INT_MAX-n).
- Length prefixes are bounded: convert_len caps at MAX_CONVERT_LEN (64 MiB); std::vector / std::map / std::vector<bool> caps element count at MAX_CONTAINER_ELEMENTS (1<<20) before resize(); deser<> rejects trailing bytes (strict).
- buf_t::convert / convert_fixed_size / convert_last all validate at_least() and reject negative sizes before alloc()+memmove; buf_t::operator+ / operator+= have explicit INT_MAX overflow asserts.
- bn_t::convert bounds value_size by MAX_SERIALIZED_BIGNUM_BYTES and INT_MAX, rejects value_size==0&&neg, and converts BN_bin2bn failure to set_error rather than throwing.
- EC point deserialization (ecc_point_t::convert_fixed_curve / from_bin) reads a fixed compressed size, validates the curve code against a whitelist (ecurve_t::convert -> ecurve_t::find), and crucially calls curve.check()/is_on_curve() after from_bin, so a malicious peer cannot inject an off-curve / wrong-subgroup point through a protocol round; the infinity-encoding path in ecurve_ossl_t::from_bin validates exact lengths and all-zero bytes.
- Protocol-level parallel-array deserialization is size-checked before indexing: ec_dkg verifies R._j.size()==n and pi_r._j.size()==n before the j*l indexing loops (R.received(j)[l] / R.received(l)[j] stay in bounds); ecdsa_mp validates ot_role_map row/column counts ==n and Qis.size()==n; PVE arrays (x_rows/r/c, X0/X1/c0/c1, b) are fixed-size kappa=SEC_P_COM C-arrays iterated by a constant, not an attacker-supplied count.

No off-by-one, integer overflow, type confusion, or unbounded-allocation primitive was reachable from the in-scope api/* round-trips. Honest zero for this cluster. Areas NOT covered by this cluster scope and worth a separate pass: the ZK proof soundness/Fiat-Shamir transcript binding (zk/*), Paillier/range-proof checks, and nonce generation — these were only skimmed where they intersected deser and are out of the deserialization mandate.

Audited in full: src/cbmpc/zk/fischlin.cpp, zk_ec.cpp, zk_paillier.cpp, zk_pedersen.cpp, zk_unknown_order.cpp, zk_elgamal_com.cpp, small_primes.cpp, plus headers include-internal/cbmpc/internal/zk/{fischlin,zk_ec,zk_paillier,zk_pedersen,zk_unknown_order,small_primes,zk_util}.h.

Reachability mapping to in-scope public APIs (include/cbmpc/api/*):
- uc_dl_t / uc_batch_dl_t (zk_ec.cpp) -> consumed by src/cbmpc/protocol/ec_dkg.cpp (proves knowledge of each party's key share x_share for Q_i), reached via ecdsa_mp.h, eddsa_mp.h, schnorr_mp.h DKG. This is the highest-value malicious-boundary path (a malicious DKG party submits uc_dl_t::verify input). 
- dh_t / uc_elgamal_com_* / elgamal_com_mult (zk_elgamal_com.cpp) -> ecdsa_mp.cpp.
- pdl_t, valid_paillier_*, two_paillier_equal_*, paillier_pedersen_equal_*, range_pedersen_* (zk_paillier.cpp / zk_pedersen.cpp) -> ecdsa_2p.cpp (keygen + refresh + sign), reached via ecdsa_2p.h.
- unknown_order_dl_t (zk_unknown_order.cpp) -> int_commitment.cpp -> ecdsa_2p.cpp.
- paillier_zero_t: no in-scope protocol consumer found (only internal); excluded.

Checks verified as PRESENT at the malicious boundary (reasons no finding was filed):
- Fischlin (uc_dl/uc_batch_dl/uc_elgamal): A committed into common_hash BEFORE challenge search (zk_ec.cpp:73, zk_elgamal_com.cpp:84); per-instance hash-zero check with b_mask (zk_ec.cpp:89); sound random-linear-combination batch relation with fresh sigma of SEC_P_STAT bits (zk_ec.cpp:82-93). Attacker-controlled params.rho/b are bounded by params.check()/check_with_effective_b enforcing b*rho>=SEC_P_COM (fischlin.h:58-75); t is not transmitted and not needed by verify. FS hashing is length-prefixed for domain separation (fischlin.h:17-22). e[i] is an int bounded by the relation+hash binding, so the absence of an explicit [0,e_max) check is not exploitable.
- All verify routines do curve.check() on every received point and check_right_open_range/check_open_range on every received scalar/ciphertext (e.g. zk_ec.cpp:66-69,80,298-299; zk_elgamal_com.cpp:75-94,167-170).
- Paillier proofs gate on flags: paillier_valid_key / paillier_no_small_factors / paillier_valid_ciphertext must be verified before dependent proofs run (zk_paillier.cpp:141,240-243,496-503). ecdsa_2p.cpp correctly chains these flags from valid_paillier_interactive before pdl/two_paillier_equal verification.
- small-prime factor rejection up to alpha (8192 for non-interactive) plus coprimality/GCD checks (zk_paillier.cpp:34,45,167,294-295; small_primes.h:11-18).
- Pedersen: subgroup check on the committed value c via check_safe_prime_subgroup (zk_pedersen.cpp:109,218,335-336); c_tilde subgroup check intentionally replaced by the documented squaring-into-QR-subgroup optimization (zk_pedersen.cpp:131); slack/range bounds on p and N enforced (zk_pedersen.cpp:107,327,333).
- FS challenges recomputed and compared against the full transcript including statement + session_id + aux in every non-interactive proof (zk_paillier.cpp:171,299,384; zk_pedersen.cpp:115,384; zk_unknown_order.cpp:66-67), so the prover cannot grind the challenge.
- unknown_order_dl_t::verify bounds z[i] bit-length (l+SEC_P_STAT+2), rejects negative z, checks b invertible and gcd(a*b,N)=1 before BN_mod_inverse (zk_unknown_order.cpp:42-53).

Conclusion: no defensible high-confidence vulnerability reachable through the in-scope public APIs was identified in this ZK cluster. Returning an honest zero rather than a speculative claim, consistent with the instruction that a wrong crypto claim is worse than none.

result.executive_summary: No findings were passed in. The FINDINGS JSON supplied to this report is an empty array, meaning zero candidate vulnerabilities survived the upstream adversarial 3-lens panel. Per the program's conservatism requirement and the instruction not to pad, there is nothing to rank or submit at this time. cb-mpc is a professionally audited library; absent a concrete, panel-validated finding with a public-API reachability path, no disclosure-grade report can responsibly be produced. This document therefore records the null result and redirects effort toward the highest-value manual-audit surfaces in include/cbmpc/api/ for the next pass.
recommended_priority_order: ecdsa_mp.h / ecdsa_2p.h signing + DKG: trace each public entry to the underlying ZK proofs and Paillier ops, looking for missing range/consistency checks on attacker-supplied protocol messages (highest payoff: key-material disclosure = Critical tier).

pve_base_pke.h, pve_batch_ac.h, pve_batch_single_recipient.h (publicly verifiable encryption): audit the verification path for soundness gaps that let a malicious party submit a ciphertext that verifies but decrypts to a different/zero value (backup/recovery key exposure).
tdh2.h (threshold decryption): check share-validation and proof-of-correct-decryption against a malicious combiner / malicious dealer using only the public API.
schnorr_mp.h / schnorr_2p.h and eddsa_*: nonce-handling and commitment-binding review (rogue-key / nonce-reuse / non-binding commitments under independent malicious party).
hd_keyset_ecdsa_2p.h / hd_keyset_eddsa_2p.h: HD derivation linkage to the 2p protocols — verify chain-code and tweak handling cannot be steered by the counterparty.
curve.h: serialization/deserialization and point-validation (invalid-curve / identity / subgroup) as a primitive reachable from all of the above.
result.coverage_and_caveats: Coverage: This report covers triage of the supplied FINDINGS JSON only, which was empty — so no code-level vulnerability analysis was performed or is being asserted. I confirmed the target exists at targets/cb-mpc and enumerated the in-scope public API surface under include/cbmpc/api/ (curve, ecdsa_2p/mp, eddsa_2p/mp, hd_keyset_ecdsa_2p, hd_keyset_eddsa_2p, pve_base_pke, pve_batch_ac, pve_batch_single_recipient, schnorr_2p/mp, tdh2). What remains uncertain: everything — no finding has been validated for reachability, exploitability, or PoC feasibility because none were submitted. Scope reminders for the next pass: demo-* directories and include/cbmpc/c_api/* are OUT OF SCOPE as sole entry points; any primitive-level bug (ZKP, commitment, Paillier, secret sharing) must be tied to a named public-API path to be eligible, and any Medium+ requires an independent-parties PoC (>=1 honest party on unmodified code, malicious party interacting only through the public protocol boundary). Recommended manual expert pass: the multi-party ECDSA signing/DKG message-validation path and the PVE verification soundness path are the two surfaces most likely to yield a defensible High/Critical and should get the deepest cryptographer review.
